paragraphs.body: My name is Joey Smith, and I am a student at the University of Pittsburgh. I recently read and admired "Girl," a mother's instruction on how her daughter should behave and the expectation of womanhood. I saw how, through the essay, the mother's voice dominates, and when the daughter does speak, the mother is dismissive. The mother thinks she is doing her best by preparing her for the reality of life. I found it interesting when, in the writing, the mother first explained to the girl how to bully a man and then how a man would bully her. Then she explained how to love a man. The mother is trying to show her that you must be powerful in a relationship before being vulnerable. The mother sees love as a secondary thing that is a luxury for a woman to have. The mother thinks power in a relationship and the ability to manipulate your partner are necessary for survival. In my own experience, my mom has taught me the opposite of what the mother in the essay teaches. My mother thinks you need to be vulnerable, you need to rely on other people, and most importantly, you need to make sure you are there for others. While I agree with my mom, I also understand where the mother in the essay comes from. The one considerable difference is that my mom is trying to raise me to become a good man, and the mother in the essay is trying to raise a good woman. The mother understands the difference between the standards for a good man and a good woman, and in her estimation, she thinks it needs to be treated as a survival skill for her daughter. I wonder why you used the informal way of writing in this essay. It was written like a list of demands, so maybe you were trying to convince people of the imperfection of the situation. Thank you for writing this essay. It made me realize the standards girls are taught to try to show that she is a good woman.

annotations.text: The mother thinks power in a relationship and the ability to manipulate your partner are necessary for survival. In my own experience, my mom has taught me the opposite of what the mother in the essay teaches.
annotations.comment: Great observation and connection! You're doing an excellent job of getting to the depths of Kincaid's writing. It's really impressive how you can celebrate a perspective so different from your own and still make the connections to your own experiences!

You're doing an excellent job of analyzing this essay and making connections between the writing an your own upbringing. I'm looking forward to seeing you make further connections between Kincaid's experiences and your own. Meanwhile, it's really great that you're addressing not only the content of the writing, but also the style--this is some high-level stuff you're doing very fluidly!
